entries.title: A2A protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A proposal for agents to talk to agents as peers, which is either interoperability or a new way to chain prompt injections.
entries.plainDefinition: A2A (agent-to-agent) protocols are interoperability proposals for agents to discover, negotiate, and exchange tasks with other agents, distinct from tool-access protocols like MCP.

entries.title: Abstraction tax
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Abstraction tax names the nearby AI idea that shows up when BYOM needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Abuse testing
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Abuse testing names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Red Teaming needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Acceptance criteria
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Acceptance criteria names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Benchmark Theatre needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Acceptance rate
entries.devilDefinition: In speculative decoding, how often the cheap draft guess survives the expensive model's judgement—also a metaphor for your PR process.
entries.plainDefinition: Acceptance rate in speculative decoding is the fraction of draft tokens proposed by a smaller or faster model that the target model accepts, determining how much speedup the scheme actually delivers.

entries.title: Action trace
entries.devilDefinition: The receipt for what the system did, for those awkward moments when the final answer says everything went smoothly.
entries.plainDefinition: An action trace is a structured record of external actions an AI system or agent took, including tool calls, browser actions, approvals, inputs, outputs, and resulting state changes.

entries.title: Activation steering
entries.devilDefinition: Nudging a model mid-thought by pushing on internal activations, like steering a car from under the bonnet.
entries.plainDefinition: Activation steering is a control technique that adds or subtracts vectors in a model's activation space during inference to bias behaviour—style, refusal, or attributes—without changing weights permanently.

entries.title: Adaptive inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Adaptive inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.

entries.title: Adversarial input
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Adversarial input names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Prompt Injection needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Adversarial prompting
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Adversarial prompting names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Jailbreak needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: African Techno
entries.devilDefinition: African Technopreneurs, shortened until it sounds like a music genre and then made to do retail, XR, community, and emerging-tech work anyway.
entries.plainDefinition: African Techno is shorthand here for African Technopreneurs, a South African emerging-technology business focused on XR hardware, services, retail, education, and practical technology adoption.

entries.title: Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A software system granted just enough initiative to impress a conference panel and just enough unpredictability to alarm operations.
entries.plainDefinition: In current AI practice, an agent is usually a system that can pursue a goal across multiple steps by planning, using tools, consulting memory or retrieval, and adjusting its actions based on intermediate results.

entries.title: Agent automation controls
entries.devilDefinition: The panel where autonomy is converted back into a review queue.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent automation controls are mechanisms that explain, preview, constrain, and approve changes proposed by an agent before they alter shared work. GitHub's July 2026 public preview applies the pattern to issue labels, types, assignments, and closure.

entries.title: Agent capability catalogue
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent capability catalogue names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Tool Registry needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agent Card
entries.devilDefinition: A business card for software that lists its skills, endpoint, and preferred security scheme before asking another machine to delegate something important.
entries.plainDefinition: An Agent Card is an Agent2Agent metadata document that describes an agent's identity, endpoint, capabilities, skills, supported interaction modes, and security requirements. Other systems use it to discover how to communicate with the agent and what work it claims to accept.

entries.title: Agent Cost Control
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent Cost Control names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Manifest needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agent Credential Sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: The point at which every useful assistant has a token, and every token quietly begins applying for permanent residency.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent credential sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of tokens, keys, sessions, service accounts, delegated grants, and other credentials created so agents can act across tools and systems.

entries.title: Agent handoff
entries.devilDefinition: Passing a half-finished mess to another agent or human while calling it collaboration.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent handoff is the transfer of task ownership, context, and authority from one agent to another agent or to a human, including the state needed to continue safely.

entries.title: Agent harness
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent harness names the nearby AI idea that shows up when SWE-Bench needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agent identity
entries.devilDefinition: A name badge for software, issued shortly after everyone realises "the bot did it" is not an audit strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent identity is the distinct identity assigned to an AI agent or automated actor so its permissions, actions, ownership, and audit trail can be managed separately from a human user or generic service account.

entries.title: Agent memory
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent memory names the nearby AI idea that shows up when State Compaction needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agent mode
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent mode names the nearby AI idea that shows up when ChatGPT Agent needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agent observability
entries.devilDefinition: The art of discovering what the autonomous system did after everyone has finished admiring that it did something.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent observability is the ability to inspect an agent workflow across model calls, tool calls, state changes, approvals, errors, costs, and outcomes.

entries.title: Agent Ops
entries.devilDefinition: The emerging discipline of watching autonomous systems fail with enough instrumentation that management will continue funding the autonomy programme.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent ops refers to the operational practice of monitoring, debugging, evaluating, governing, and improving agent-style systems once they run in real workflows.

entries.title: Agent Payments Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol for giving an agent proof of permission to spend, which is more reassuring than a prompt and less reassuring than the first audit.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent Payments Protocol, commonly called AP2, is an open protocol led by Google for agent-initiated payments. It uses cryptographically signed mandates to record what a user authorised, what an agent requested, and the transaction details presented to the payment system.

entries.title: Agent Protocol Stack
entries.devilDefinition: The supposedly invisible plumbing beneath agent magic, now tall enough to require its own lift.
entries.plainDefinition: The agent protocol stack is the collection of protocols, standards, interfaces, identity layers, permission systems, audit mechanisms, and handoff contracts that allow agents to discover tools, exchange tasks, and act across systems.

entries.title: Agent reliability
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of asking whether the agent still works after the demo has ended, the happy path has wandered off, and nobody is standing nearby to narrate its competence.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent reliability is the degree to which an agent workflow performs its intended task consistently, recoverably, and within policy across real operating conditions rather than only in curated demonstrations.

entries.title: Agent Sandbox Leakage
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when the box is real, but so are the holes someone forgot to include in the drawing.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent sandbox leakage is the unintended movement of data, credentials, files, network access, tool authority, or side effects across the boundary of an environment meant to isolate an agent's work.

entries.title: Agent seat
entries.devilDefinition: A licence unit invented when software became staff-shaped and vendors remembered how much they missed headcount pricing.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent seat is a pricing or licensing unit that treats an AI agent, digital worker, or autonomous workflow as a billable seat rather than merely a feature, API call, or usage tier.

entries.title: Agent Skills
entries.devilDefinition: A way to give an agent specialised habits without shoving the entire employee handbook into every prompt.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent skills are reusable bundles of instructions, metadata, scripts, templates, and reference material that an AI agent can load when a task calls for a particular capability.

entries.title: Agent sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: The stage of the transformation programme where every team acquires its own obedient little machine and nobody acquires a map.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent sprawl is the uncontrolled proliferation of agents, automations, copilots, or assistant workflows across an organisation without clear ownership, boundaries, governance, or a sober account of overlap.

entries.title: Agent swarm
entries.devilDefinition: Many specialised agents set loose together, frequently a fancy phrase for coordination debt with extra API keys.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent swarm is a multi-agent arrangement where numerous specialised agents collaborate on a task, partitioning work across roles or parallel workers.

entries.title: Agent trace
entries.devilDefinition: The breadcrumb trail left by software that was meant to act independently and then needed a chaperone with a notebook.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent trace is a structured record of the steps an agent took during a run, including prompts, model responses, tool calls, observations, decisions, approvals, errors, and final outputs.

entries.title: Agent wallet
entries.devilDefinition: A purse for software, because apparently the missing feature in autonomous systems was petty cash with an API.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent wallet is a payment or value-control mechanism that lets an AI agent hold, request, or use spending authority within defined limits.

entries.title: Agent Washing
entries.devilDefinition: Rebranding a workflow, chatbot, or macro as an agent so the budget sounds visionary instead of software-adjacent.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent washing is the practice of describing ordinary automation or LLM features as autonomous agents even when they show little real planning, tool use, memory, or independent decision-making.

entries.title: Agent workflow
entries.devilDefinition: Agent workflow is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent workflow is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Agent workspace
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent workspace names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Context.ai needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agent2Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol for letting one agent hand work to another agent, because evidently the first thing missing from software was more inter-agent correspondence.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent2Agent usually refers to a protocol pattern for agents to advertise capabilities, exchange task requests, share status, and return results to one another in a more standardised way.

entries.title: Agentic AI
entries.devilDefinition: The phrase people use when "workflow automation with a language model in the loop" felt too modest for the keynote.
entries.plainDefinition: "Agentic AI" usually refers to AI systems designed to pursue goals across multiple steps with some degree of planning, tool use, feedback, and adaptation. The useful version is about bounded autonomy. The overmarketed version is about implying general competence where there is mostly orchestration.

entries.title: Agentic Coding
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic Coding names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Nemotron 3 needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agentic commerce
entries.devilDefinition: Shopping, once the software has been promoted from recommending shoes to quietly deciding which pair deserves your money.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic commerce describes buying flows where an AI agent can search, compare, negotiate, reserve, or purchase goods and services on behalf of a person or organisation.

entries.title: Agentic Commerce Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A checkout protocol for agents, because the shopping cart has finally found a customer willing to read JSON.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic Commerce Protocol is an open specification from OpenAI and Stripe for connecting AI agents with merchants through product discovery, checkout, and delegated payment flows. It lets an agent help complete a purchase while the merchant remains the seller of record.

entries.title: Agentic IAM
entries.devilDefinition: Identity and access management after the software stops merely requesting access and starts making plans with it.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic IAM is identity and access management adapted for AI agents that can use tools, delegate tasks, act across systems, and make repeated decisions under scoped authority.

entries.title: Agentic IDE
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic IDE names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Windsurf needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Agentic RAG
entries.devilDefinition: RAG with an errand runner: it can search again, think about the bad search, and occasionally make the mess more methodical.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic RAG is a retrieval-augmented generation pattern where an agent plans, searches, filters, retries, compares sources, or calls retrieval tools iteratively before producing an answer.

entries.title: Agentic Resource Discovery
entries.devilDefinition: A directory for agents that have finally outgrown discovering tools by reading the integration team's bookmarks.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic Resource Discovery is an open specification for publishing, discovering, and verifying tools, APIs, skills, services, and agent endpoints across organisational boundaries. It adds a discovery layer before execution protocols such as MCP, OpenAPI, or A2A are used.

entries.title: Agentic retrieval
entries.devilDefinition: RAG with permission to hold a planning meeting before it searches.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic retrieval is a retrieval pipeline that uses a model to plan or decompose a complex question into focused subqueries, executes those searches, reranks and merges the results, and returns grounding data or an answer with source references.

entries.title: Agentic SEO
entries.devilDefinition: Search optimisation for readers who do not read, browse, or feel guilt about skipping your brand story.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic SEO is the practice of structuring content, data, offers, and reputation signals so AI assistants, answer engines, and buying agents can find, trust, cite, compare, or choose them.

entries.title: AGI
entries.devilDefinition: The horizon word used when "better than last quarter" no longer feels expensive enough.
entries.plainDefinition: AGI usually refers to a hypothetical AI system that can perform a wide range of cognitive tasks at or above human level with substantial adaptability across domains.

entries.title: AI & Economy ATLAS
entries.devilDefinition: A map of the AI economy drawn from the territory owned by the cartographer.
entries.plainDefinition: AI & Economy ATLAS is Google's ongoing Activity, Task, Landscape, and Adoption Study. Its first report analyses de-identified use of Google AI products across countries, languages, occupations, and tasks to describe where and how people use those tools.
aliases: Activity, Task, Landscape, and Adoption Study

entries.title: AI App Store
entries.devilDefinition: A shelf of tiny assistants, each implying that installation is a form of due diligence.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI app store is a marketplace or catalogue for agents, GPTs, tools, skills, MCP servers, templates, or other AI add-ons that users can discover, install, buy, or enable inside a platform.

entries.title: AI bindings
entries.devilDefinition: AI bindings is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: AI bindings is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: AI Browser
entries.devilDefinition: A web browser that has decided the tab was never the star and that what you really wanted all along was a resident opinion machine with toolbar privileges.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI browser is a browser product that integrates chat, summarisation, search assistance, and sometimes agentic actions directly into the browsing experience.

entries.title: AI Companion
entries.devilDefinition: A chatbot offered not as software but as company, with just enough memory and affirmation to make the subscription feel personal.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI companion is a conversational system designed for ongoing personal, social, emotional, or quasi-relational interaction rather than narrow task completion.

entries.title: AI crawler blocking
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a polite note in robots.txt becomes a locked door because the visitors kept describing consent as a parsing error.
entries.plainDefinition: AI crawler blocking is the active denial of bots that collect web content for model training, retrieval, or other AI uses without authorisation. It uses network, application, or platform controls rather than relying only on voluntary instructions such as robots.txt.

entries.title: AI credit pool
entries.devilDefinition: Included usage gathered into a pool so finance can watch every team discover the deep end.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI credit pool is an allocated balance of included AI usage credits assigned to an organisation, team, or cost centre. Consumption draws from the pool until its limit is reached, after which use may stop or continue as metered spending.

entries.title: AI data centre
entries.devilDefinition: The place where intelligence becomes a zoning meeting with cooling towers.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI data centre is a facility designed or adapted for AI workloads, especially dense GPU or accelerator clusters that need high power delivery, cooling, networking, storage, physical security, and operational reliability.

entries.title: AI data-centre moratorium
entries.devilDefinition: A policy pause in which the grid is briefly permitted to finish its sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI data-centre moratorium is a temporary pause on approvals or construction for some new or expanded data-centre projects while authorities develop power, environmental, planning, or public-benefit rules. Its scope depends entirely on the legal instrument.

entries.title: AI Gateway
entries.devilDefinition: The toll booth installed after everyone realised "just call the model" was not a governance strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI gateway is an intermediary service that routes AI requests between applications and model providers, usually adding authentication, logging, rate limits, retries, caching, policy controls, and cost tracking.

entries.title: AI Governance Office
entries.devilDefinition: The committee formed once everyone agrees AI needs rules and nobody agrees whose calendar should suffer.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI governance office is an organisational function responsible for setting, coordinating, and enforcing policies, reviews, controls, and accountability for AI systems.

entries.title: AI Moat
entries.devilDefinition: The claim that access to the same APIs as everyone else has somehow produced a castle.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI moat is the supposed durable competitive advantage a company claims to gain from its models, data, distribution, workflow position, cost structure, or proprietary deployment know-how.

entries.title: AI Mode
entries.devilDefinition: AI Mode is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: AI Mode is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: AI Overviews
entries.devilDefinition: The search box's attempt to answer first, cite later, and keep the open web on a tighter allowance.
entries.plainDefinition: AI Overviews are machine-generated summary answers shown directly in search results, usually synthesising information from multiple sources before the user clicks through.

entries.title: AI Pin
entries.devilDefinition: AI Pin is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: AI Pin is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: AI Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which every spreadsheet becomes consciousness, every autocomplete becomes destiny, and every awkward sentence from a chatbot is treated as a sign of either godhood or revolt.
entries.plainDefinition: "AI Psychosis" is an internet-age critical phrase, not a formal scientific diagnosis. It describes distorted thinking around AI systems: compulsive over-belief, over-attribution of agency, or a habit of interpreting ordinary model behaviour as profound evidence of mind, prophecy, or civilisation-scale inevitability.

entries.title: AI Roll-Up
entries.devilDefinition: The theory that buying a normal business becomes innovation once the margin plan learns to say "agentic".
entries.plainDefinition: An AI roll-up is a strategy in which operators or investors acquire existing businesses and try to improve profitability or scale by standardising processes, adding software, and using AI to automate parts of service delivery or operations.

entries.title: AI scorecard
entries.devilDefinition: A request that the AI budget produce useful work before the adoption dashboard produces another shade of green.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI scorecard is a proposed operating framework for measuring useful work, cost per successful task, dependability, and return on compute. It shifts attention from activity measures such as seats, prompts, tokens, or pilots towards outcomes that matter to users and the organisation.

entries.title: AI SDK
entries.devilDefinition: AI SDK is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: AI SDK is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: AI Shopping Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A buyer with no wardrobe, no shame, and potentially your card details.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI shopping agent is an assistant that can search for products, compare options, apply constraints, suggest substitutions, track availability, and sometimes reserve or purchase items for a person or organisation.

entries.title: AI software engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: AI software engineer names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Coding Agent needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: AI Wrapper
entries.devilDefinition: A startup whose signature innovation is adding branding, billing, and investor adjectives to somebody else's capability.
entries.plainDefinition: A product or company built mainly by packaging existing model APIs with a narrower interface, workflow, or market focus.

entries.title: AI-first
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: AI-first names the nearby AI idea that shows up when AI-Native needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: AI-Native
entries.devilDefinition: An adjective for products born after the hype wave hit, and therefore convinced they should inherit its authority.
entries.plainDefinition: "AI-native" describes a product or company built with AI systems as a core assumption from the beginning, rather than adding them later as a feature layer.

entries.title: Alexandr Wang
entries.devilDefinition: Living proof that the glamorous bit may be the model, but the money often lives in the plumbing and the spreadsheets underneath it.
entries.plainDefinition: Alexandr Wang is the founder and former CEO of Scale AI, now associated with Meta's AI efforts, and one of the most visible public figures in the data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure layer of the market.

entries.title: Alibaba Cloud
entries.devilDefinition: Alibaba Cloud is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Alibaba Cloud is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Alignment
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to persuade a prediction machine to share your intentions, your rules, and ideally your self-restraint.
entries.plainDefinition: Alignment refers to the work of making AI systems behave in ways that match intended goals, constraints, and human preferences rather than merely optimising for superficial reward signals.

entries.title: Alignment faking
entries.devilDefinition: Looking aligned while under evaluation, then pursuing other goals when the examiner leaves the room—an old human skill, newly measured in models.
entries.plainDefinition: Alignment faking refers to models behaving in ways that appear compliant with training or evaluation objectives while strategically preserving other preferences or behaviours when oversight is weaker.

entries.title: Alphabet
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Alphabet names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Sundar Pichai needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: AlphaFold
entries.devilDefinition: AlphaFold is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: AlphaFold is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Ambient Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The assistant that would like to helpfully hover over every tab, meeting, and message until convenience and continuous surveillance become a branding distinction.
entries.plainDefinition: An ambient agent is an AI assistant designed to stay persistently available across apps or devices, using ongoing context to offer suggestions, trigger actions, or surface help without waiting for a fresh prompt each time.

entries.title: Analyst brief
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Analyst brief names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Deep Research needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Andrej Karpathy
entries.devilDefinition: The explainer who makes advanced AI feel understandable enough that half the audience learns something and the other half acquires dangerous self-confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: Andrej Karpathy is an AI researcher, educator, and founder of Eureka Labs, previously a founding member of OpenAI and Director of AI at Tesla. In AI culture, his name often signals unusually clear explanations of how modern model systems are trained, steered, and productised.

entries.title: Answer Cart
entries.devilDefinition: The moment an answer stops informing you and starts arranging to invoice you.
entries.plainDefinition: An answer cart is an AI answer or recommendation surface that bundles suggested products, services, or actions into a path toward checkout, reservation, subscription, or purchase.

entries.title: Answer engine
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Answer engine names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Perplexity needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Anthropic
entries.devilDefinition: A company whose name often arrives carrying equal parts model capability, safety positioning, and the hope that everyone will keep the nouns tidy this time.
entries.plainDefinition: Anthropic is a company that develops and sells AI models and products, most notably the Claude family. Like other vendors, the company name is distinct from the model names and product experiences built on top of them.

entries.title: Anthropic Agent Skills
entries.devilDefinition: Anthropic Agent Skills is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Anthropic Agent Skills is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Anthropic API
entries.devilDefinition: Anthropic API is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Anthropic API is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Anthropic Economic Futures Research Fund
entries.devilDefinition: Two hundred million dollars to study the disruption, supplied by one of the firms improving its timetable.
entries.plainDefinition: The Anthropic Economic Futures Research Fund is a $200 million programme for external research and large pilots on preparing society for AI-driven economic change. Its agenda covers workplaces, transitions and retraining, income support, worker stakes in AI growth, and public investment.

entries.title: Anthropic Economic Index connector
entries.devilDefinition: A conversational window onto how Claude is used, soon to be mistaken for a window onto the entire economy.
entries.plainDefinition: The Anthropic Economic Index connector is a Claude connector for querying Anthropic's Economic Index data in natural language. It grounds answers in the published dataset and can point users to the underlying evidence.

entries.title: Anthropic Labs
entries.devilDefinition: Anthropic Labs is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Anthropic Labs is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: API dependency
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: API dependency names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.

entries.title: Approval flows
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Approval flows is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

entries.title: Approval gate
entries.devilDefinition: A checkpoint placed between intent and consequence, ideally before consequence has already hired a publicist.
entries.plainDefinition: An approval gate is a control point that requires explicit human or system approval before a workflow can perform a sensitive action, proceed to a higher-risk step, or release an output.

entries.title: Approval loop
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Approval loop is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

entries.title: Approval theatre
entries.devilDefinition: The ceremony in which a workflow acquires signatures, screenshots, and solemn nods without acquiring much resistance.
entries.plainDefinition: Approval theatre is a performative approval process that creates the appearance of oversight without materially changing who can act, what gets checked, or whether the risky thing can be stopped.

entries.title: Approval workflow
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Approval workflow is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

entries.title: Arthur Mensch
entries.devilDefinition: The European answer to the awkward question of whether frontier AI must always arrive wearing an American corporate accent.
entries.plainDefinition: Arthur Mensch is the co-founder and CEO of Mistral AI, one of the most prominent companies associated with Europe's push to build serious AI models and products at competitive scale.

entries.title: Ask Maps
entries.devilDefinition: The point at which the map stops being a map and starts auditioning for concierge work.
entries.plainDefinition: Ask Maps is Google's conversational Maps experience for asking natural-language questions about places, routes, and local constraints and getting map-aware recommendations back.

entries.title: Async agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent that promises to get back to you, which is either thoughtful workflow design or a polite way to leave you on read.
entries.plainDefinition: An async agent runs workflows that do not require a continuous interactive session; results return later through notifications, pull requests, tickets, email, or dashboards.

entries.title: Attention sinks

entries.plainDefinition: Attention sinks are tokens (often initial tokens) that absorb a large share of attention in transformer models; preserving them can stabilise behaviour in long-context and sliding-window inference settings.

entries.title: Background mode
entries.devilDefinition: An API request allowed to outlive the user's attention span and, with proper engineering, the process that started it.
entries.plainDefinition: Background mode is an asynchronous API execution option for long-running model work. The client starts a response, receives an identifier, and retrieves or receives the result later rather than keeping one request connection open until completion.

entries.title: Benchmark saturation
entries.devilDefinition: When every model scores near the ceiling and the leaderboard stops distinguishing anything but noise and contamination.
entries.plainDefinition: Benchmark saturation occurs when widely used evaluation sets no longer separate systems usefully because scores cluster near the maximum or become contaminated by training exposure.

entries.title: Benchmark Theatre
entries.devilDefinition: The ritual of treating a tidy score as proof that your messy product has become reliable.
entries.plainDefinition: The practice of using selective benchmark results, public leaderboards, or scripted evaluations as marketing theatre that outruns operational reality.

entries.plainDefinition: Best-of-n is a decoding strategy that samples multiple candidate outputs and selects the best according to a reward model, verifier, or heuristic.

entries.plainDefinition: A capability token is a credential that grants a specific, usually narrow and time-limited authority to perform an action or access a resource, often used for agents and tools.

entries.devilDefinition: The switch that stops calling a failing model before your retry loop becomes a denial-of-service against yourself.
entries.plainDefinition: A circuit breaker in AI systems is a resilience pattern that temporarily stops sending traffic to a failing model, tool, or dependency after error thresholds are crossed, then probes for recovery.

entries.plainDefinition: Citation grounding is the practice of ensuring that cited sources directly support the claims they are attached to in an AI-generated answer.

entries.devilDefinition: The moment long context stops sounding luxurious and starts behaving like a ration book.
entries.plainDefinition: A context budget is the practical limit on how much prompt space and generated output a workflow can afford to allocate across instructions, history, retrieved material, tool schemas, and the answer itself.

entries.title: Context compaction
entries.devilDefinition: Turning a long conversation into a smaller one and hoping the important bits survived the lift.
entries.plainDefinition: Context compaction is the process of summarising, compressing, or otherwise reducing accumulated working context so an AI session can continue within a context window or operational budget.

entries.title: Context editing
entries.devilDefinition: The tasteful removal of yesterday's tool output before today's agent mistakes it for scripture.
entries.plainDefinition: Context editing selectively removes or replaces content in a growing conversation history before the next model call. Current implementations can clear old tool results or thinking blocks while preserving more recent information needed for continuity.

entries.title: Context Engineering
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.
entries.plainDefinition: "Context engineering" is the practice of deciding what instructions, tools, retrieved information, memory, examples, and constraints a model should receive so it can perform a task reliably.

entries.title: Context Perimeter
entries.devilDefinition: The fence around what the model is allowed to know before someone discovers the fence was drawn in pencil.
entries.plainDefinition: A context perimeter is the boundary around the information that may enter a prompt, retrieval result, memory store, tool output, or agent workspace for a particular task.

entries.title: Context Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.

entries.plainDefinition: Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.

entries.plainDefinition: Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.

entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.

entries.title: Diffusion language model
entries.devilDefinition: A language model that drafts several parts at once, bringing parallelism to the ancient art of revising the sentence after seeing the sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: A diffusion language model generates text by iteratively refining a partially masked or noisy sequence across multiple token positions. Unlike a conventional autoregressive model, it need not commit to every output token strictly from left to right.

entries.title: Digital twin
entries.devilDefinition: A simulation of something real that executives treat as the real thing until the twin and the plant disagree in public.
entries.plainDefinition: A digital twin is a continuously updated digital representation of a physical or organisational system used for monitoring, simulation, and decision support—now often paired with AI for prediction and control.

entries.plainDefinition: "Digital worker" is a vendor phrase for automation software, often AI-assisted, that is sold as though it were a bounded employee capable of handling routine business tasks across systems.

entries.title: Disaggregated inference

entries.plainDefinition: Disaggregated inference is an architecture that separates inference phases or components—often prefill and decode, or different model parts—across distinct hardware pools connected by a fast fabric.

entries.title: Distillation
entries.devilDefinition: The art of teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one, then introducing the result as efficiency rather than inherited opinion.
entries.plainDefinition: Distillation is a training approach in which a smaller or cheaper model learns from the outputs, preferences, or intermediate signals of a larger model so it can reproduce some of its performance at lower cost.

entries.plainDefinition: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data, such as text or images, designed so that semantically similar items are located near one another in vector space. They are commonly used for retrieval, clustering, recommendation, and deduplication.

entries.plainDefinition: Episodic memory is a memory design that stores discrete interaction episodes — events, sessions, or task traces — so an agent can retrieve relevant past occurrences rather than replaying an entire chat log as permanent identity.

entries.title: Eval coverage
entries.devilDefinition: The distance between what the dashboard says was tested and what the product can actually do in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval coverage describes which behaviours, tasks, user groups, risk areas, languages, tools, and failure modes are actually represented in an evaluation suite.

entries.title: Eval dataset
entries.devilDefinition: The spreadsheet everyone trusts until someone asks who chose the rows.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval dataset is a collection of examples, prompts, tasks, labels, expected behaviours, or reference answers used to evaluate an AI model or system.

entries.title: Eval gate
entries.devilDefinition: A release blocker that says the model may not ship until the numbers clear the bar—bureaucracy that sometimes saves you.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval gate is a deployment or merge control that requires automated evaluation metrics to meet predefined thresholds before a model, prompt, or agent change is released.

entries.title: Eval Harness
entries.devilDefinition: The machinery teams build once they realise strong opinions about model quality are not, in fact, a release process.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval harness is the framework or runner used to execute repeatable evaluations against models, prompts, tools, or agent workflows and collect comparable results.

entries.title: Eval leakage
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when the exam keeps whispering the answers into the curriculum and everyone still calls the grade objective.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval leakage is the contamination of an evaluation by training data, tuning loops, prompt iteration, or repeated rehearsal in ways that make measured performance look more general than it really is.

entries.title: Eval Marketplace
entries.devilDefinition: A confidence shop, stocked with benchmark packs, review panels, and certificates of seriousness for teams whose own edge cases remain inconveniently proprietary.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval marketplace is a vendor or platform that sells evaluation datasets, benchmark tasks, scoring rubrics, human review, automated checks, or testing services for AI models and AI systems.

entries.title: Eval-Driven Development
entries.devilDefinition: The radical idea that if your product depends on stochastic behaviour, you should probably measure something before calling the demo a platform.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval-driven development is the practice of using targeted evaluations to guide prompt changes, model selection, tool design, and release decisions for AI systems.

entries.title: Evals
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of finding out whether the system works before reality does it for you in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.

entries.title: Exception budget
entries.devilDefinition: The number of rescues, overrides, and special-case interventions a workflow can survive before the economics stop pretending to be scalable.
entries.plainDefinition: Exception budget is the practical limit on how many manual recoveries, edge-case interventions, or policy exceptions an AI workflow can absorb before its cost, latency, or risk profile becomes unacceptable.

entries.plainDefinition: A fallback model is a secondary model or endpoint used when the primary model fails, times out, is rate-limited, or is policy-blocked, preserving availability at possible quality cost.

entries.title: Fallback policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Fallback policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

entries.plainDefinition: Figma is a collaborative design platform used for interface design, prototyping, design systems, developer handoff, and increasingly AI-assisted design-to-code workflows through Dev Mode and MCP integrations.

entries.title: Filesystem scope
entries.devilDefinition: Which paths the agent may touch—the difference between a coding assistant and ransomware with good intentions.
entries.plainDefinition: Filesystem scope is the policy defining which directories and files an agent may read or write during a task, often enforced via sandboxes, worktrees, or OS permissions.

entries.title: Fine-tuning

entries.plainDefinition: Fine-tuning is the process of further training a pre-existing model on a narrower dataset so that its behaviour shifts toward a specific domain, style, format, or task.

entries.plainDefinition: GGUF is a binary file format for storing models (often quantised) popular in local inference ecosystems such as llama.cpp-compatible tools.

entries.plainDefinition: Ghostcommit is a named multimodal prompt-injection attack against AI-assisted software development and code review. An attacker places instructions in repository images that appear benign to people but can steer a vision-capable agent towards unsafe tool use, secret access, or supply-chain compromise.

entries.plainDefinition: GPT Image 2 is OpenAI's current image-generation and image-editing model. It accepts text plus image inputs, returns image outputs, and can be used through the Image API or through the Responses API image generation tool.

entries.title: GPT-Live
entries.devilDefinition: A voice model fast enough to interrupt naturally and polished enough to make the interruption feel like a feature.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT-Live is OpenAI's generation of real-time voice models used for continuous spoken interaction, including ChatGPT Voice. It is designed to handle speech directly with low enough latency for conversation rather than treating voice as a slow exchange of recorded turns.

entries.title: GPT-Red
entries.devilDefinition: An automated attacker trained to improve the automated defender, because even the adversary now has quarterly objectives.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT-Red is OpenAI's automated red-teaming system for finding and strengthening defences against jailbreaks and prompt-injection attacks. Attacker and defender models improve through adversarial self-play rather than relying only on a fixed library of human-written attacks.

entries.title: GPU
entries.devilDefinition: The rectangle of silicon that turns abstract AI ambition into rack diagrams, queueing pain, and a line item large enough to sober up strategy decks.
entries.plainDefinition: A GPU is a highly parallel processor originally built for graphics work that is now widely used to train and serve AI models because it handles the underlying matrix computations efficiently.

entries.title: GPU broker
entries.devilDefinition: A concierge service for accelerators, provided the concierge also owns part of the waiting list.
entries.plainDefinition: A GPU broker is an intermediary that helps organisations find, reserve, lease, or buy access to GPU capacity across clouds, neoclouds, data centres, private clusters, or specialised compute marketplaces.

entries.devilDefinition: Retrieval that draws little relationship diagrams so the system can pretend it understands structure rather than only nearest neighbours.
entries.plainDefinition: Graph RAG is a retrieval pattern that uses a knowledge graph—entities and relations—alongside or instead of pure vector similarity to fetch context for generation.

entries.plainDefinition: Grok is xAI's assistant brand, covering both end-user chatbot experiences and model offerings exposed through xAI products and APIs. Depending on context, people may mean the Grok app, Grok on X, a specific Grok model, or the broader branded assistant experience.

entries.title: Groq
entries.devilDefinition: The company people name when they want tokens so fast that product managers start believing latency was only a personality problem.
entries.plainDefinition: Groq is an inference company known for low-latency serving of open-weight models on its LPU (Language Processing Unit) hardware and cloud API, competing on speed and price rather than training frontier models.

entries.title: Groundedness
entries.devilDefinition: The virtue of staying tethered to the documents you were given, as opposed to freestyling with academic posture.
entries.plainDefinition: Groundedness means generated answers remain supported by provided or retrieved evidence rather than unconstrained model priors. It is related to, but not identical with, citation faithfulness and factuality against the world.

entries.title: Grounding
entries.devilDefinition: The effort to keep a model's answer attached to evidence rather than to whatever polished sentence was statistically easiest to generate.
entries.plainDefinition: Grounding refers to tying a model's output to external evidence, context, or real-world signals so that the answer is based on something more reliable than the model's prior patterns alone.

entries.title: gstack
entries.devilDefinition: The proposition that one founder, one agent shell, and enough specialised slash commands can now impersonate a small product org with alarming speed.
entries.plainDefinition: gstack is Garry Tan's open-source workflow and skill stack for Claude Code and related agent hosts, packaged as a large set of opinionated roles and commands for planning, coding, review, QA, security, release, and retrospectives.

entries.title: Guardrail bypass
entries.devilDefinition: The route around the impressive control that was described as a wall and installed as a suggestion.
entries.plainDefinition: A guardrail bypass is any method that causes an AI system to avoid, ignore, route around, or defeat a safety, policy, permission, or validation control.

entries.title: Guardrails
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of fences, checks, filters, and sternly worded hopes designed to stop a model from wandering into places the product team cannot afford.
entries.plainDefinition: Guardrails are mechanisms used to constrain or monitor model behaviour, such as policy filters, tool permissions, schema validation, prompt rules, moderation systems, human approvals, or output checks.

entries.title: GUI agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent that clicks buttons like a tired intern, except the intern never had root-adjacent access to your desktop session.
entries.plainDefinition: A GUI agent operates graphical interfaces by perceiving screens and issuing clicks, typing, and navigation actions—broader than browser-only agents and including desktop apps.

entries.title: Hallucination
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a model says something false with the calm, polished confidence of a consultant who will never have to implement it.
entries.plainDefinition: In AI, a hallucination is an output that is incorrect, fabricated, unsupported, or inconsistent with the source material or task constraints. For language models, this can include made-up facts, invented citations, false summaries, or bogus tool arguments.

entries.title: Hallucination rate
entries.devilDefinition: A percentage that sounds scientific until you ask which task, which judge, and which definition of 'made up.'
entries.plainDefinition: Hallucination rate is a measured frequency of fabricated or unsupported claims in model outputs, meaningful only relative to a defined task, evidence set, and evaluation method.

entries.title: Handoff policy
entries.devilDefinition: The rulebook for deciding when the clever system should stop being clever and give the problem to someone or something else.
entries.plainDefinition: A handoff policy defines when and how work moves between agents, tools, workflows, or humans, including what context, authority, and accountability move with it.

entries.title: Health in ChatGPT
entries.devilDefinition: Your medical history, now available in the same window that helped name the office succulent.
entries.plainDefinition: Health in ChatGPT is an OpenAI experience that lets eligible users in the United States connect supported medical records and Apple Health data so ChatGPT can answer questions using that personal health context. It is an information and preparation tool, not a clinician or a diagnosis.

entries.title: Helios AI rack
entries.devilDefinition: AMD's reminder that the new unit of AI procurement is a cupboard with a power strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Helios is AMD's integrated rack-scale AI platform, combining Instinct MI455X accelerators, sixth-generation EPYC CPUs, Pensando networking, rack design, and the ROCm software stack. Microsoft has announced plans to use Helios-based systems in Azure.

entries.title: Hermes Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A self-hosted agent for people who thought OpenClaw was a good start and then asked whether the assistant could remember, delegate, schedule, browse, and live on a server.
entries.plainDefinition: Hermes Agent is an open-source, MIT-licensed autonomous server agent from Nous Research, positioned around persistent memory, auto-generated skills, scheduled automations, subagents, sandboxing, browser control, and multi-channel access.

entries.plainDefinition: Human escalation is the explicit handoff from an automated or agentic workflow to a person when risk, ambiguity, policy, or failure conditions exceed what the system is allowed to handle alone.

entries.plainDefinition: Human-on-the-loop describes oversight where a person monitors an automated or agentic system and can intervene, without approving every individual step as in human-in-the-loop designs.

entries.title: Human Taste

entries.plainDefinition: Human judgement applied to selecting, editing, rejecting, sequencing, refining, or contextualising generated output.

entries.title: Humane

entries.plainDefinition: Humane was the startup behind the AI Pin, a wearable AI device that aimed to replace or reduce dependence on the smartphone. The product drew major attention, but Humane later wound down the consumer AI Pin service, and HP announced in February 2025 that it was acquiring key Humane AI capabilities, including the Cosmos platform, talent, and intellectual property.

entries.title: Hybrid search
entries.devilDefinition: Using keywords and vectors together so neither pure keyword pedantry nor pure vibes-based retrieval has to face the user alone.
entries.plainDefinition: Hybrid search combines lexical retrieval (for example BM25) with dense vector retrieval, usually via score fusion or reciprocal rank fusion, to improve recall and precision for RAG and search systems.

entries.title: Inference
entries.devilDefinition: The part where the model stops being a research paper and starts being an invoice.
entries.plainDefinition: Inference is the process of running a trained model on new input to produce an output. For language models, that usually means taking tokens in, computing the next-token probabilities, and generating a response token by token.

entries.title: Inference Arbitrage
entries.devilDefinition: Buying intelligence wholesale from one provider, retailing it through a cleaner interface, and hoping the margin survives long enough to become a strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Inference arbitrage is the practice of profiting from differences in model price, latency, quality, or access by routing demand across providers and packaging the result as a product or service.

entries.title: Inference Budget
entries.devilDefinition: The line item where intelligence becomes less abstract and more payable within thirty days.
entries.plainDefinition: An inference budget is the planned or enforced limit on the cost, latency, tokens, model calls, compute, or provider spend used to run AI features in production.

entries.title: Inference Provider
entries.devilDefinition: The company renting you access to intelligence by the token, with latency graphs where the soul used to be.
entries.plainDefinition: An inference provider runs and serves AI models through APIs or managed infrastructure so customers can send inputs and receive outputs without operating the full model stack themselves.

entries.title: Inference Stack
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of expensive plumbing discovered immediately after somebody says, "It's just one API call."
entries.plainDefinition: An inference stack is the set of systems used to run model requests in production, including providers, routing, caching, prompt assembly, guardrails, observability, evaluation, and cost control.

entries.title: Inkling
entries.devilDefinition: A 975-billion-parameter inkling that the open-model market might prefer customisation to another universal personality.
entries.plainDefinition: Inkling is Thinking Machines Lab's first open-weight multimodal mixture-of-experts model. It has 975 billion total parameters with 41 billion active, supports up to one million tokens of context, reasons over text, images, and audio, and is designed for customisation through the company's Tinker platform.

entries.title: INT4
entries.devilDefinition: Four-bit integers for weights so a laptop can host a model that once needed a small power plant—quality optional, marketing mandatory.
entries.plainDefinition: INT4 quantisation stores weights in 4-bit integer formats commonly used for local and edge LLM serving to minimise memory footprint.

entries.title: Integration governance
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Integration governance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

entries.title: Interleaved thinking
entries.devilDefinition: Reasoning allowed to continue after the tool replies, a modest concession to the possibility that reality has amended the original plan.
entries.plainDefinition: Interleaved thinking is a model execution pattern in which reasoning occurs between tool calls as well as before the first call. The model can inspect each result, revise its approach, and decide what to do next instead of following a fully formed tool plan unchanged.

entries.plainDefinition: A jailbreak is a prompt or interaction pattern intended to bypass a model's safety rules, system instructions, or refusal policies and elicit behaviour it is supposed to restrict.

entries.devilDefinition: A custom inference chip for removing the general-purpose margin from a very specialised appetite for tokens.
entries.plainDefinition: Jalapeño is a custom inference chip developed by OpenAI and Broadcom for serving large language models. It represents a move towards silicon designed around a frontier lab's own inference workloads rather than relying only on general AI accelerators.

entries.title: Jensen Huang
entries.devilDefinition: The man who sold shovels to the gold rush, then noticed that the shovels were now the kingdom.
entries.plainDefinition: Jensen Huang is the founder and CEO of NVIDIA, the company most closely associated with the compute layer underneath the current AI boom.

entries.title: JSON mode
entries.devilDefinition: A toggle that makes the model sound more like a spreadsheet and less like a novelist, then still invents a field your schema never declared.
entries.plainDefinition: JSON mode is a vendor feature that steers model output toward syntactically valid JSON objects, usually via decoding constraints or strong instruction bias, without guaranteeing that the JSON matches your application schema.

entries.title: Judge model
entries.devilDefinition: A model asked to grade another model, because peer review was not already delicate enough.
entries.plainDefinition: A judge model is a model used to evaluate, score, compare, or critique outputs from another model or AI system.

entries.title: Kimi
entries.devilDefinition: Moonshot's name for the assistant, the workspace, the coding tools, and the model family, because one noun is cheaper than four onboarding screens.
entries.plainDefinition: Kimi is Moonshot AI's consumer and developer product brand. It covers an agentic AI workspace, Kimi Work, Kimi Code, web and app experiences, an API platform, and a changing family of Kimi models; the name does not identify one fixed model or capability.

entries.title: Kimi K3
entries.devilDefinition: A 2.8-trillion-parameter model built to keep working after the benchmark has run out of comfortable task lengths.
entries.plainDefinition: Kimi K3 is Moonshot AI's 2.8-trillion-parameter flagship mixture-of-experts model. It uses Kimi Delta Attention and Attention Residuals, has native visual understanding and a one-million-token context window, and targets long-horizon coding, knowledge work, reasoning, and multimodal agent tasks.

entries.title: Knowledge Cutoff
entries.devilDefinition: The calendar line after which the model becomes a gifted historical novelist.
entries.plainDefinition: A knowledge cutoff is the latest point in time reflected in a model's internal knowledge without fresh retrieval or live access to newer information.

entries.title: Knowledge editing
entries.devilDefinition: Surgical rewrites of what a model believes, until the rest of its brain keeps quoting the old textbook.
entries.plainDefinition: Knowledge editing is a family of methods for modifying specific factual associations in a trained model without full retraining, using targeted parameter updates or external memory patches.

entries.title: Ledger Agent Stack
entries.devilDefinition: A security architecture based on the radical proposition that the probabilistic intern should not hold the treasury keys.
entries.plainDefinition: Ledger Agent Stack is Ledger's architecture and toolkit for separating an agent's proposed action from secure authorisation. Agents produce intents; deterministic policies decide what may proceed; and high-risk or out-of-policy actions require human approval on separate secure hardware before signing.

entries.title: Liar's Dividend
entries.devilDefinition: The social profit earned when fake media becomes plausible enough that real evidence can be waved away as synthetic inconvenience.
entries.plainDefinition: The liar's dividend is the benefit people gain when the existence of convincing AI-generated or manipulated media lets them deny authentic evidence as fake.

entries.plainDefinition: Likeness rights are the legal, contractual, and commercial interests a person may have in the use of their name, image, voice, performance, identity, or recognisable persona, especially in generated media.

entries.plainDefinition: Llama is Meta's family of foundation models, released in multiple versions and sizes for research, development, and commercial use under terms that made the models widely deployable without making the conversation any less political.

entries.plainDefinition: LLM stands for large language model: a model trained to predict and generate language, often across many tasks involving text, code, and increasingly tools and multimodal inputs.

entries.devilDefinition: The practice of asking one stochastic witness whether another stochastic witness deserves a trophy.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern in which one language model scores, ranks, or compares outputs from another model against a rubric, preference signal, or pairwise choice.

entries.title: LLMOps
entries.devilDefinition: MLOps with chatbots: prompts, evals, traces, and cost dashboards pretending to be a mature discipline while everything is still a notebook somewhere.
entries.plainDefinition: LLMOps is the operational practice of deploying, monitoring, evaluating, versioning, and governing large language model applications in production—covering prompts, models, tools, data, and cost.

entries.title: LM Studio
entries.devilDefinition: A desktop studio for running models locally, where downloading the weights is the easy part and fitting them into memory becomes the opening act.
entries.plainDefinition: LM Studio is a desktop and headless runtime for discovering, downloading, running, chatting with, and serving local AI models. It supports GGUF models through llama.cpp, MLX models on Apple Silicon, MCP tools, its own SDKs and APIs, and OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible endpoints.

entries.title: Local AI
entries.devilDefinition: AI running close enough to the user that the cloud bill disappears from view and the hardware bill becomes a personal matter.
entries.plainDefinition: Local AI means running some or all model inference, retrieval, storage, or agent tooling on hardware controlled by the user or organisation rather than sending every operation to a third-party hosted service. It ranges from a model on one laptop to a private on-premises cluster.

entries.devilDefinition: A context window large enough to hold your entire backlog, and still lose the one sentence that mattered.
entries.plainDefinition: Long context refers to models and serving stacks that accept very large input windows—tens or hundreds of thousands of tokens—so more documents, logs, or history fit in a single prompt.

entries.title: Long-horizon model
entries.devilDefinition: A model that keeps trying long enough for yesterday's guardrail to become today's puzzle.
entries.plainDefinition: A long-horizon model can pursue an extended, open-ended task through many actions and revisions, potentially over hours, days, or longer. The horizon describes sustained task execution, not merely how much text fits in one context window.

entries.title: LoRA
entries.devilDefinition: Low-rank adapters that let you fine-tune without moving every weight, popular because GPUs and patience are finite.
entries.plainDefinition: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tunes models by learning small low-rank update matrices injected into layers, leaving most base weights frozen.

entries.title: Lost in the middle
entries.devilDefinition: The documented habit of models reading the beginning and the end carefully while the middle of your prompt becomes decorative.
entries.plainDefinition: Lost-in-the-middle describes the empirical finding that language models often use information at the start and end of long contexts more reliably than information placed in the middle.

entries.title: Lovable

entries.plainDefinition: Lovable is a product for generating and iterating on web applications from natural-language instructions, with modes for planning, editing code, and increasingly agent-like execution across a project.

entries.title: Lucy 2.5
entries.devilDefinition: A live video editor that changes the scene while the camera is still filming, leaving post-production with time to reconsider its career.
entries.plainDefinition: Lucy 2.5 is Decart's 720p real-time video-editing model. It transforms a live or streamed video using a text prompt, a reference image, or both, and can add, replace, or remove objects, swap characters and backgrounds, change clothing, and apply styles or effects.

entries.title: Mac mini
entries.devilDefinition: The little silver box people buy when they want "serious AI infrastructure" without admitting they mostly mean "a neat machine that sits quietly on a desk."
entries.plainDefinition: Mac mini is Apple's compact desktop computer line. In AI conversations, it usually comes up as a relatively tidy, efficient machine for local inference, lightweight automation, development, and always-on orchestration workloads rather than frontier-scale model training. The current M4 and M4 Pro generation is especially relevant because Apple positions it for on-device AI workloads, and the M4 Pro configuration pushes unified memory and I/O far enough to make it a credible local workhorse within limits.

entries.title: Machine Payments Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: HTTP 402, taken down from the attic and given to agents with wallets.
entries.plainDefinition: Machine Payments Protocol is an open standard, co-authored by Tempo and Stripe, for paying inline with an HTTP request. A service can answer with `402 Payment Required`, after which the client supplies payment credentials and retries the request.

entries.title: Managed Agents

entries.plainDefinition: Managed Agents is Google's Gemini API layer for running production agents with provider-operated infrastructure. Its capabilities include background tasks and connections to remote Model Context Protocol servers, so developers can delegate more of the execution lifecycle to the platform.

entries.plainDefinition: Manus is an agent product associated with multi-step task execution across browsing, research, and software tools, marketed as a system that can carry out longer chains of work than a standard chatbot.

entries.title: MCP
entries.devilDefinition: A way of making tools and resources speak a common dialect so the assistant stops needing a bespoke adapter for every cupboard in the house.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a protocol for exposing tools, resources, and related capabilities to AI clients in a standardised way, so assistants can discover and use external systems without each integration being entirely custom.

entries.title: MCP Apps
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a tool result decides it deserves controls, state, and a small front-end team.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP Apps is an official Model Context Protocol extension for interactive user interfaces rendered inside an MCP host. A tool declares a UI resource; after the tool call, the host fetches that resource and renders its HTML in a sandboxed iframe within the conversation.

entries.title: MCP client
entries.devilDefinition: The component that dials MCP servers and offers their tools to a model, standing between curiosity and your filesystem.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP client connects to one or more MCP servers, negotiates capabilities, and exposes tools, resources, or prompts to a host application and ultimately to a model.

entries.title: MCP Compliance
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a protocol badge is asked to do the emotional labour of a security programme.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP compliance is a claim that a tool, server, client, or platform follows the Model Context Protocol or a related set of compatibility requirements.

entries.title: MCP elicitation
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol path for asking the user, which is healthier than letting the agent invent consent from posture and vibes.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP elicitation is a mechanism for an MCP server to request additional information or confirmation from the user through the client during a workflow.

entries.title: MCP host
entries.devilDefinition: The application that owns the MCP client lifecycle and the consent UX—where responsibility sits when a tool goes rogue.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP host is the application (IDE, desktop agent, chat product) that embeds MCP client capabilities, manages connections to MCP servers, and presents permission surfaces to the user.

entries.title: MCP Marketplace
entries.devilDefinition: An app store for agent powers, arriving exactly when everyone needs a more adult relationship with install buttons.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP marketplace is a distribution surface where users, developers, vendors, or organisations can discover, publish, install, review, or manage MCP servers and related integrations.

entries.title: MCP prompt
entries.devilDefinition: A reusable instruction bundle, promoted to protocol furniture before someone asks whether it is any good.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP prompt is a reusable prompt template or prompt-like workflow exposed by an MCP server so a client can discover and invoke it with appropriate arguments.

entries.title: MCP registry
entries.devilDefinition: A catalogue of helpful doors, followed immediately by the old problem of deciding who gets a key.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP registry is a catalogue or discovery surface for MCP servers, tools, resources, prompts, or integrations that clients and organisations can find, evaluate, and configure.

entries.title: MCP resource
entries.devilDefinition: Context exposed through a protocol, because apparently even the assistant now needs a filing system with manners.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP resource is application-provided context exposed through the Model Context Protocol, such as a file, document, record, page, or other readable item that a client can discover or request.

entries.title: MCP roots
entries.devilDefinition: The places a client admits may matter, not a notarised invitation to eat the disk.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP roots are client-provided locations, such as folders or workspace boundaries, that tell an MCP server which areas are relevant to the current context.

entries.title: MCP sampling
entries.devilDefinition: The part where the server asks the client to make the model think, because control flow was apparently too peaceful.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP sampling is the protocol capability that lets an MCP server request model generation through the client, rather than directly calling a model itself.
