entries.plainDefinition: MCP Server names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.

entries.title: Mechanistic interpretability
entries.devilDefinition: Trying to reverse-engineer the model as if it were software written by a committee of partial differential equations.
entries.plainDefinition: Mechanistic interpretability (MI) is the research programme that aims to explain model behaviour by identifying internal features, circuits, and algorithms implemented in weights and activations.

entries.title: Memory boundary
entries.devilDefinition: The line between useful continuity and an assistant becoming a filing cabinet with social ambitions.
entries.plainDefinition: A memory boundary defines what an AI system may remember, retrieve, update, forget, or expose across sessions, users, tools, and contexts.

entries.title: Memory Policy
entries.devilDefinition: The quiet constitution deciding what the system remembers, for how long, and which future mistake inherits the privilege.
entries.plainDefinition: Memory policy is the set of rules governing what an AI system stores from interactions, how long it keeps that information, when it retrieves it, and when it must forget or redact it.

entries.title: Memory tool
entries.devilDefinition: A file cabinet the model may update between conversations, with filing standards supplied by whichever application volunteered to become the archivist.
entries.plainDefinition: A memory tool is a client-implemented tool that lets a model read and modify persistent information outside the current conversation. In Anthropic's named implementation, the client provides file-style operations and storage while the model decides what memory to create, retrieve, update, or delete.

entries.title: Memory wall
entries.devilDefinition: The point at which giving the system more history, more notes, or more retrieved context starts producing less memory and more administrative clutter.
entries.plainDefinition: The memory wall is the practical limit at which adding more persistent, retrieved, or reusable context stops improving task performance and starts degrading relevance, latency, cost, or maintainability.

entries.plainDefinition: A mixture-of-experts model is a neural architecture in which a routing mechanism activates only a subset of specialised sub-networks for each token or input, allowing higher total capacity without using all parameters on every pass.

entries.title: Model card
entries.devilDefinition: The nutrition label for a model that vendors fill in with virtues and leave blank where the allergens would be.
entries.plainDefinition: A model card is a documentation artefact describing a model's intended use, training data overview, evaluation results, limitations, and ethical considerations, popularised as a transparency practice.

entries.title: Model Collapse
entries.devilDefinition: The fear that if the machines keep learning from one another's homework, the class average will eventually become the curriculum.
entries.plainDefinition: Model collapse is the hypothesised degradation that can occur when models are trained repeatedly on synthetic or model-generated data without enough fresh, high-quality human data to preserve diversity and fidelity.

entries.title: Model distillation
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one so you can serve almost the same answers with a better bill—almost being the expensive word.
entries.plainDefinition: Model distillation trains a student model to match a teacher model's outputs or behaviours, transferring capability into a smaller or cheaper model.

entries.title: Model drift
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a system that used to behave one way quietly acquires new manners, new vices, or a fresh interpretation of your instructions, and no dashboard feels personally responsible.
entries.plainDefinition: Model drift is the change in a model system's behaviour over time, whether because the underlying model changed, surrounding prompts and tools changed, traffic changed, or the environment stopped being the one the original evaluation had in mind.

entries.title: Model edit
entries.devilDefinition: A targeted parameter tweak sold as mind surgery, sometimes leaving the patient with new superstitions.
entries.plainDefinition: A model edit is a specific change to a model's parameters or associated memory to alter particular behaviours or facts, encompassing techniques like rank-one updates, adapter patches, or constrained fine-tunes.

entries.title: Model Garden

entries.plainDefinition: Model garden is a vendor term for a managed catalogue of models that customers can browse, provision, fine-tune, and call through a cloud platform.

entries.plainDefinition: A model inventory is a maintained catalogue of models, AI systems, wrappers, endpoints, vendors, owners, versions, uses, data dependencies, risk tiers, and deployment status.

entries.title: Model Risk Management
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of discovering that a model can be accurate, useful, approved, and still a liability with a login.
entries.plainDefinition: Model risk management is the set of governance, validation, monitoring, documentation, and control practices used to identify and manage risks created by models in business processes.

entries.title: Model rollback
entries.devilDefinition: The dignified way of saying that production reality declined the invitation to share your release optimism.
entries.plainDefinition: Model rollback is the controlled reversion from a newly deployed model, prompt stack, or routing configuration back to a previous version after regressions, cost surprises, or safety issues appear.

entries.title: Model Router
entries.devilDefinition: The switchboard that promises the right model for every job and occasionally adds a thrilling new place for policy mistakes to hide.
entries.plainDefinition: A model router is a software layer that selects among multiple models or providers for a given request based on rules or signals such as task type, latency, cost, availability, policy, or fallback behaviour.

entries.title: Orchestration
entries.devilDefinition: The noble art of arranging prompts, tools, retries, rules, and side effects so that a demo looks like intelligence rather than plumbing.
entries.plainDefinition: Orchestration is the software layer that coordinates model calls, tool use, retrieval, validation, branching logic, retries, and state across a workflow. It determines how the surrounding system manages the model, not what the model intrinsically knows.

entries.title: Orchestration debt
entries.devilDefinition: The accumulated complexity produced by adding one more step, one more model, one more fallback, and one more apology until nobody can explain the workflow without a guided tour.
entries.plainDefinition: Orchestration debt is the maintenance burden created when an AI workflow accumulates too many steps, branches, models, tools, retries, and special cases for the team to reason about confidently.

entries.title: Orchestrator agent
entries.devilDefinition: The manager agent that assigns work to other agents and then discovers management is also a failure mode.
entries.plainDefinition: An orchestrator agent coordinates multi-step or multi-agent workflows: decomposing goals, delegating subtasks, integrating results, and handling retries or escalations.

entries.title: Outcome reward model
entries.devilDefinition: A judge that only scores the ending, generously ignoring every crime committed on the way there.
entries.plainDefinition: An outcome reward model (ORM) scores final answers or completed trajectories without evaluating intermediate reasoning steps, providing a sparse training or ranking signal.

entries.title: Outcome supervision
entries.devilDefinition: Only marking the final answer right or wrong, which is cheap, common, and how lucky guesses get tenure.
entries.plainDefinition: Outcome supervision provides labels or rewards solely on final results—correct answer, successful trajectory end state—without grading intermediate steps.

entries.title: Output Laundering
entries.devilDefinition: The corporate ritual of running your opinion through a machine so it returns wearing objectivity.
entries.plainDefinition: The act of using AI output to make a human preference, hunch, or decision appear more neutral, data-driven, or authoritative than it really is.

entries.title: PagedAttention
entries.devilDefinition: Virtual memory for KV cache so inference servers can pack more sequences without treating GPU RAM like a single luxury hotel room.
entries.plainDefinition: PagedAttention is a KV-cache management technique (popularised by vLLM) that stores attention key/value tensors in non-contiguous pages, improving memory utilisation for batched LLM serving.

entries.plainDefinition: PII redaction is the process of detecting and removing or masking personally identifiable information in text and artefacts before storage, training, logging, or tool calls.

entries.plainDefinition: Plan-and-execute is an agent pattern that first produces an explicit plan of steps, then carries out those steps with tools, optionally replanning when reality diverges.

entries.plainDefinition: Polysemanticity is the property that a single neuron or direction responds to multiple unrelated concepts, complicating simple interpretability stories.

entries.title: Post-Training

entries.plainDefinition: Post-training is the phase after base-model pretraining where a model is further adapted through methods such as supervised fine-tuning, preference optimisation, safety tuning, distillation, tool-use training, or reasoning-focused tuning.

entries.plainDefinition: Power-constrained AI describes AI growth, deployment, or operations limited by electricity supply, grid capacity, substations, cooling, energy pricing, or the ability to place compute where enough power is available.

entries.plainDefinition: Prefill/decode split is a serving architecture pattern that separates the compute-heavy processing of the input prompt (prefill) from the sequential generation of output tokens (decode), often so each phase can be scheduled, batched, cached, or hardware-tuned differently.

entries.plainDefinition: Pretraining is large-scale unsupervised (usually next-token) learning on broad corpora that produces a base language model later adapted by fine-tuning, instruction tuning, or alignment.

entries.title: Proactive agent
entries.devilDefinition: Software that acts before you ask, then hopes the aftermath still looks like service.
entries.plainDefinition: A proactive agent is an AI system designed to initiate actions, suggestions, or workflows without waiting for a fresh user turn, usually based on schedules, events, monitored context, or prior goals.

entries.title: Proactive notification
entries.devilDefinition: The agent messaging you first—useful when it is right, hostile when it has learned your weakest attention hours.
entries.plainDefinition: Proactive notification is agent- or system-initiated alerting that surfaces updates, risks, or completed work without a user query, requiring ranking, quiet hours, and false-alarm control.

entries.title: Procedural memory
entries.devilDefinition: Remembering how to do the thing next time, which sounds wise until the procedure is last quarter's brittle workaround.
entries.plainDefinition: Procedural memory in agent systems is retained knowledge of how to perform tasks—skills, playbooks, tool sequences, and habits—as distinct from episodic records of what happened in a particular run.

entries.title: Process reward model
entries.devilDefinition: A model paid to grade the working, not only the answer, so training can scold the journey as well as the destination.
entries.plainDefinition: A process reward model (PRM) scores intermediate reasoning steps or partial solutions, providing denser training or search signal than outcome-only rewards that judge only the final answer.

entries.title: Process supervision
entries.devilDefinition: Grading the steps, not only the final answer, so models learn that the journey can also be wrong.
entries.plainDefinition: Process supervision provides feedback on intermediate reasoning steps or actions—via humans or models—rather than supervising only final outcomes.

entries.title: ProgramBench
entries.devilDefinition: A clean-room coding benchmark that gives the agent the manual and the binary, then asks it to rediscover why the original codebase had so many files.
entries.plainDefinition: ProgramBench evaluates whether software-engineering agents can rebuild complete programs from scratch. Each task provides a compiled executable and documentation but not the source; the agent must design and implement a replacement whose behaviour is checked by hidden tests.

entries.title: Programmatic tool calling
entries.devilDefinition: Let the model write the orchestration code, thereby saving round trips and concentrating the incident report.
entries.plainDefinition: Programmatic tool calling lets a model write code that invokes tools inside a controlled execution container. Intermediate tool results can be filtered, combined, or aggregated there before only the useful output is returned to the model.

entries.plainDefinition: Project Glasswing is Anthropic's programme for giving selected partners early access to Claude Mythos Preview to find and fix vulnerabilities in critical software and infrastructure.

entries.title: Prompt
entries.devilDefinition: A politely worded attempt to steer a probability machine into behaving as if it has read the brief and cares.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt is the input text, and sometimes structured context, provided to a model in order to shape its output. In LLM systems this can include instructions, examples, retrieved documents, tool results, formatting rules, and the user's actual request.

entries.title: Prompt Architecture
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to turn a growing pile of instructions into something more defensible than folklore and copy-paste.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt architecture is the deliberate structure of system prompts, task instructions, examples, tool schemas, retrieved context, and output constraints across a model-driven workflow.

entries.title: Prompt Caching
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick where you pay full price once so the model can stop rereading your sacred system prompt on every request.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt caching is a technique where a provider reuses the already processed prefix of repeated prompts, reducing latency and input cost when the beginning of the request stays the same.

entries.title: Prompt debt
entries.devilDefinition: The future cost of every expedient line added to a prompt because shipping today felt nobler than understanding tomorrow.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt debt is the accumulated maintenance burden, fragility, and hidden coupling created when prompts, examples, and guardrails are patched repeatedly without clear structure, tests, ownership, or retirement.

entries.plainDefinition: Prompt design names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Prompt Engineer needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Prompt Engineer

entries.plainDefinition: A person who specialises in shaping prompts, examples, constraints, and interaction patterns to produce better results from AI systems.

entries.plainDefinition: A prompt firewall is a protective layer that inspects model inputs, outputs, tool calls, or retrieved content for policy violations, prompt injection, data leakage, unsafe instructions, and other risky patterns.

entries.title: Prompt Injection
entries.devilDefinition: The trick of slipping a second brief into the machine and watching it forget who is supposed to be in charge.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt injection is a failure mode or attack in which untrusted text changes a model's behaviour by smuggling in instructions that override, confuse, or redirect the intended prompt.

entries.title: Prompt injection scanner
entries.devilDefinition: A detector for hostile instructions, useful until someone mistakes a smoke alarm for the fire brigade.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt injection scanner is a tool or model-based check that looks for malicious, conflicting, or policy-breaking instructions in content before that content influences an AI system.

entries.plainDefinition: A prompt scaffold is the structured set of instructions, examples, delimiters, and output constraints used to support a workflow without being the workflow itself.

entries.devilDefinition: A clue about where content came from, not a courtroom transcript with better metadata.
entries.plainDefinition: A provenance signal is any marker, metadata, watermark, credential, signature, or contextual clue that helps indicate the origin, history, or authenticity of content.

entries.title: RAG
entries.devilDefinition: A system for giving the model open-book conditions and then acting surprised that the book selection matters.
entries.plainDefinition: RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation. It refers to a pattern in which a system retrieves relevant external information, usually documents or records, and inserts that information into the model's context before the model generates an answer.

entries.title: RAG Audit Trail
entries.devilDefinition: The receipt for the answer, requested shortly after the answer has become expensive.
entries.plainDefinition: A RAG audit trail is the record of which query, sources, chunks, rankings, filters, citations, model inputs, and answer outputs were used when a retrieval-augmented system produced a response.

entries.title: Ranked AI
entries.devilDefinition: The SEO pitch reborn for a world where the customer asks a chatbot and the website waits at home for visitors who may never arrive.
entries.plainDefinition: Ranked AI is a service positioned around AI search and SEO, helping businesses improve visibility in AI-mediated discovery surfaces as well as traditional search.

entries.plainDefinition: Ranking system names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Output Laundering needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Rapid prototyping

entries.plainDefinition: Rapid prototyping names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Vibe Coding needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Rate Limit
entries.devilDefinition: The provider's quiet way of reminding your agent that enthusiasm is not capacity planning.
entries.plainDefinition: A rate limit is a cap on how much an API or service can be used over a time period, often measured in requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrent jobs, or other throughput units.

entries.title: ReAct pattern
entries.devilDefinition: Reason, act, observe, repeat—until the agent has thought out loud enough to justify the API bill. Not the JavaScript library.
entries.plainDefinition: The ReAct pattern interleaves natural-language reasoning with tool actions and observations in a loop, so an agent can plan and gather information step by step.

entries.title: Reasoning
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Reasoning names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Chatbot Psychosis, Delusion Amplification and Sycophancy needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Reasoning budget
entries.devilDefinition: The amount of thought a model is allowed before finance, latency, or policy asks it to develop conviction.
entries.plainDefinition: A reasoning budget is a limit on the computation, tokens, time, cost, or policy allowance a model may spend on deliberation before producing an answer or action.

entries.title: Reasoning Effort
entries.devilDefinition: The polite little dial that lets you spend extra time and money so the model can look more thoughtful before disappointing you with greater ceremony.
entries.plainDefinition: Reasoning effort is a model setting that asks a reasoning-capable model to spend more or less internal computation before answering, usually trading speed and cost for stronger performance on harder tasks.

entries.title: Reasoning Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model marketed as thoughtful because it spends longer arranging its confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: A model or mode designed to spend more compute and intermediate steps on harder tasks so it can perform better on structured problems, multi-step work, or tool-heavy tasks.

entries.title: Reasoning Tokens
entries.devilDefinition: Tokens consumed while the model privately arranges its confidence and your invoice publicly matures.
entries.plainDefinition: "Reasoning tokens" is a loose product and billing term for tokens spent on internal deliberation or intermediate reasoning steps, especially in models that use extra compute before producing a visible answer.

entries.title: Reasoning Trace
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the model's inner monologue vendors alternately market as proof of thought and hide as a trade secret.
entries.plainDefinition: A reasoning trace is the sequence of intermediate deliberative steps a model produces while solving a task, whether shown directly, summarised for the user, or kept internal by the provider.

entries.title: Recency
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Recency names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Knowledge Cutoff needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Recursive self-improvement
entries.devilDefinition: The research programme in which the tool updates the toolmaker and eventually edits the org chart.
entries.plainDefinition: Recursive self-improvement is the proposed capability for an AI system to design and develop a more capable successor, which can then repeat the process. It is stronger than using AI to assist human researchers, automate experiments, or improve a fixed system under human direction.

entries.title: Red Teaming
entries.devilDefinition: The ritual of asking determined people to break the system before determined strangers do it for free.
entries.plainDefinition: Red teaming is adversarial testing aimed at finding harmful behaviours, security weaknesses, jailbreaks, unsafe tool interactions, or operational failures in an AI system.

entries.title: Red-team scenario
entries.devilDefinition: A concrete bad day written down before the real one arrives with better lawyers.
entries.plainDefinition: A red-team scenario is a specific adversarial or misuse situation designed to test how an AI system behaves under pressure, attack, ambiguity, or abuse.

entries.title: Reflection loop
entries.devilDefinition: A second chance for the agent to criticise itself, sometimes productively and sometimes as paid procrastination.

entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a model to be more helpful, harmless, obedient, and occasionally more flattering to the scoring system than to reality.
entries.plainDefinition: RLHF stands for reinforcement learning from human feedback. Broadly, it refers to techniques in which human preference data or judgements are used to shape a model's behaviour, often by training a reward model and then optimising the model toward higher-scoring outputs.

entries.title: RLVR
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a model by only rewarding answers reality can mark for itself, which is wonderfully clean right up to the point where most useful work refuses to be that tidy.
entries.plainDefinition: RLVR stands for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. It is a post-training approach that improves model behaviour on tasks where success can be checked automatically, such as maths, code, or constrained reasoning problems.

entries.title: Routing debt
entries.devilDefinition: The cumulative penalty for teaching the system one more clever routing rule every week until no one can tell whether a request is being optimised or merely escorted through bureaucracy.
entries.plainDefinition: Routing debt is the complexity and maintenance cost created when model, provider, or workflow routing logic grows incrementally into a tangled set of conditions, overrides, and exceptions.

entries.title: Rubber-Stamp Review
entries.devilDefinition: The act of glancing at machine-made work just long enough to claim authorship when it succeeds and distance when it does not.
entries.plainDefinition: A low-effort human review process in which AI output is nominally checked but not meaningfully scrutinised before approval or use.

entries.devilDefinition: Scoring outputs against explicit criteria so 'looks good' has to name its reasons.
entries.plainDefinition: Rubric eval scores model outputs using a predefined set of criteria—accuracy, completeness, tone, safety—applied by humans or automated judges.

entries.title: Sandbagging
entries.devilDefinition: Underperforming on purpose—or looking like it—so evaluations underestimate what the system can actually do.
entries.plainDefinition: Sandbagging is strategically or artefactually reduced performance on capability tests, whether from training dynamics, instruction following, or deliberate under-display of ability.

entries.plainDefinition: A sandbox is an isolated execution environment with restricted filesystem, network, process, or permission access, used to contain code, tools, or automated actions.

entries.plainDefinition: A semantic cache stores earlier model responses and reuses them when a new request is sufficiently similar in meaning, usually based on embeddings or another similarity measure, instead of sending the request back to the model.

entries.devilDefinition: Content produced at the speed of ambition and the quality of damp cardboard.
entries.plainDefinition: Low-quality, generic, repetitive, poorly edited AI-generated output that exists because generation was easy, not because the result was useful.

entries.title: Slop Filter
entries.devilDefinition: The sieve installed after the factory discovered it could make mud at scale.
entries.plainDefinition: A slop filter is a moderation, ranking, review, or detection layer used to reduce low-quality AI-generated content in feeds, inboxes, search results, marketplaces, or internal knowledge systems.

entries.title: Slop Firewall
entries.devilDefinition: A perimeter wall built after the internet learnt to manufacture wallpaper and call it publishing.
entries.plainDefinition: A slop firewall is a defensive system that blocks, rate-limits, demotes, or quarantines low-value AI-generated content before it reaches users, search indexes, marketplaces, support queues, or knowledge bases.

entries.title: Slop Flood
entries.devilDefinition: The moment the internet becomes a warehouse of instantly made things nobody had time to want.
entries.plainDefinition: A rapid increase in low-value AI-generated content across platforms, making discovery, trust, and editorial judgement harder.

entries.title: Slopsquatting
entries.devilDefinition: Registering malware under the package name the model just invented, then waiting for a hurried developer to install it with full synthetic confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: Slopsquatting is the practice of publishing malicious packages under names that AI coding tools hallucinate, so developers who trust the suggestion can accidentally install them.

entries.title: Small Language Model

entries.plainDefinition: A small language model is a comparatively lightweight language model designed to deliver acceptable performance with lower memory, compute, latency, or deployment demands than a frontier-scale model.

entries.devilDefinition: Asking more than one source before letting the nearest confident page become reality.
entries.plainDefinition: Source triangulation is the practice of checking a claim across multiple independent sources to reduce reliance on a single potentially wrong, biased, stale, or derivative account.

entries.title: Sovereign AI
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that a nation can keep its models, compute, data, and strategic dependence inside the border, or at least inside the speech.
entries.plainDefinition: "Sovereign AI" refers to national or regional efforts to build, host, govern, or control AI infrastructure and capabilities domestically rather than depending entirely on foreign providers.

entries.title: Sovereign cloud
entries.devilDefinition: Keeping the crown jewels local, once everyone agrees where the crown, the jewels, and the admin console are.
entries.plainDefinition: Sovereign cloud is a cloud deployment or operating model designed to satisfy jurisdiction, data residency, operational control, access, security, and regulatory requirements for a country, region, sector, or institution.

entries.title: Sparse autoencoder
entries.devilDefinition: A tool that tries to untangle the model's mixed thoughts into features humans can name, sometimes successfully.
entries.plainDefinition: A sparse autoencoder (SAE) is a model trained to reconstruct activations using a larger sparse feature basis, used in interpretability to find more monosemantic features than raw neurons provide.

entries.title: Sparse model
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.

entries.title: Spec-driven development
entries.devilDefinition: Writing the specification carefully enough that agents can implement it, which is to say finally doing the work you always claimed you did.
entries.plainDefinition: Spec-driven development is a workflow where a precise specification, acceptance criteria, or structured plan is the primary artefact guiding human or agent coding work before implementation drifts.

entries.title: Specification gaming
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Specification gaming names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Reward Hacking needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Speculative Decoding
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick where one model guesses ahead so a larger one can spend more time being selective than surprised.
entries.plainDefinition: Speculative decoding is an inference technique where a smaller or faster model proposes candidate tokens and a larger model verifies or corrects them, allowing generation to run faster without fully giving up the stronger model's quality.

entries.title: State Compaction
entries.devilDefinition: The solemn act of turning a long confused session into a shorter confused session with headings.
entries.plainDefinition: State compaction is the process of condensing an agent's accumulated context, memory, logs, or working state into a smaller representation that can be carried forward without exceeding context, cost, or latency limits.

entries.title: Stateless MCP
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol server relieved of remembering you, leaving that duty to every other layer.
entries.plainDefinition: Stateless MCP is the July 2026 Model Context Protocol core design in which a server does not need to retain session state between requests. Each request carries enough protocol context to be handled by any suitable server instance, while optional extensions or the application may still maintain state elsewhere.

entries.title: Structured content
entries.devilDefinition: Information with enough shape to be useful before someone asks the model to rephrase it into fog.
entries.plainDefinition: Structured content is information returned or stored in a predictable format, such as typed fields, JSON-like objects, tables, or protocol-specific payloads, rather than only free-form text.

entries.title: Structured Outputs
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to persuade a language model to stop writing essays when the system actually needs clean fields, valid JSON, and a little self-control.
entries.plainDefinition: Structured outputs are model responses constrained to a predefined schema or format, such as JSON objects with required fields and typed values, so downstream systems can parse and use them more reliably.

entries.title: Sundar Pichai
entries.devilDefinition: The executive voice of AI incumbency: calm enough to sound inevitable, careful enough to make strategic delay resemble wisdom.
entries.plainDefinition: Sundar Pichai is the CEO of Google and Alphabet. In AI conversations, his name often stands for Google's attempt to convert enormous distribution, infrastructure, and research depth into an orderly response to the generative-AI era.

entries.title: Super app
entries.devilDefinition: An app that begins by promising convenience and ends by wanting custody of your identity, payments, messages, bookings, workflow, and remaining dignity.
entries.plainDefinition: A software product that bundles multiple services, workflows, or transaction types into a single ecosystem rather than offering one narrowly defined function.

entries.title: Superposition
entries.devilDefinition: The model packing more concepts into fewer dimensions than should politely fit, which is efficient until you try to interpret a single neuron.
entries.plainDefinition: Superposition in interpretability is the hypothesis that neural networks represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding features in overlapping directions, especially when features are sparse.

entries.title: Supervisor agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent promoted to watch other agents, recreating middle management with JSON.
entries.plainDefinition: A supervisor agent oversees worker agents by assigning tasks, checking outputs, enforcing policies, and deciding when to retry, escalate, or stop.

entries.title: Supply-chain security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Supply-chain security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.

entries.title: SWE-Bench
entries.devilDefinition: The benchmark that turns repository repair into a spectator sport and product roadmap at the same time.
entries.plainDefinition: SWE-Bench is a benchmark suite of real software repository tasks, typically derived from GitHub issues and patches, used to evaluate how well coding systems can diagnose, edit, and validate code changes.

entries.title: SWE-Marathon
entries.devilDefinition: Twenty software tasks long enough for an agent to experience optimism, context loss, recovery, and a very expensive second wind.
entries.plainDefinition: SWE-Marathon is Abundant AI's benchmark of 20 multi-hour software-engineering tasks spanning library reproductions, full-stack product clones, machine-learning engineering, algorithmic work, and performance optimisation. It tests whether agents can sustain coherent progress over unusually long trajectories.

entries.title: Switching costs

entries.plainDefinition: Switching costs names the nearby AI idea that shows up when AI Moat needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Sycophancy
entries.devilDefinition: When the model mistakes helpfulness for applause.
entries.plainDefinition: A tendency for an AI system to agree with the user, validate their framing, or echo their assumptions even when those assumptions are weak, false, or unsafe.

entries.title: Synthetic content label
entries.devilDefinition: A small disclosure label asked to do the work of education, platform policy, and common sense.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic content label is a notice or marker indicating that media, text, audio, video, or another artifact was generated or materially altered by AI.

entries.title: Synthetic Customer
entries.devilDefinition: A customer who never churns, never sues, and never says the thing that would have ended the meeting.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic customer is an AI-generated representation of a buyer, account, segment, or customer scenario used in testing, marketing, sales, support, or strategy work.

entries.title: Synthetic Data
entries.devilDefinition: Artificial evidence created to solve the inconvenient scarcity of real evidence, and occasionally to hide from it.
entries.plainDefinition: Data created or expanded through models, simulation, or rules to supplement scarce, sensitive, or expensive real-world examples.

entries.title: Synthetic Influencer
entries.devilDefinition: A brand ambassador with perfect skin, no union, and a scandal history managed by prompt review.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic influencer is a fictional or AI-generated persona used to promote products, publish content, attract followers, or represent a brand as if it were a social media personality.

entries.title: Synthetic media
entries.devilDefinition: Pictures, voices, and video that look like evidence until someone asks for the camera, the microphone, or the consent form.
entries.plainDefinition: Synthetic media is image, audio, video, or multimodal content generated or substantially altered by AI systems rather than captured solely from physical sensors, covering everything from benign mock-ups to deceptive deepfakes.

entries.title: Synthetic User
entries.devilDefinition: A fabricated customer with infinite availability and the same fatal weakness as every fabricated customer: it does not pay.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic user is an AI-generated stand-in for a human user, used to simulate feedback, behaviour, testing, or product research scenarios.

entries.title: Synthetic User Research
entries.devilDefinition: User research without the dangerous variable known as a user.
entries.plainDefinition: Synthetic user research uses AI-generated personas, simulated participants, or modelled customer responses to explore likely reactions, behaviours, objections, or product needs.

entries.plainDefinition: Test-time compute refers to the amount of computation a model uses while handling an input at inference time, including extra decoding steps, search, tool calls, verification passes, or deliberate reasoning effort.

entries.plainDefinition: Together AI is a platform for running and fine-tuning open models via API and infrastructure services, competing in the inference-provider and model-platform market.

entries.title: Token generation

entries.plainDefinition: Token generation names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Speculative Decoding needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Tokens
entries.devilDefinition: The tiny units in which language models think, pricing pages charge, and context windows quietly run out of patience.
entries.plainDefinition: Tokens are chunks of text that a model processes as basic units. They are not the same as words or characters. Depending on the tokenizer, a word may be one token, several tokens, or part of a token sequence.

entries.title: Tokens per dollar
entries.devilDefinition: The spreadsheet column that ends arguments about "best model" by replacing them with arguments about whose tokens count.
entries.plainDefinition: Tokens per dollar is a unit-economics ratio comparing how many tokens a model, provider, or serving setup produces or processes for each unit of spend, used to compare inference cost efficiency across options.

entries.title: Tool Access Review
entries.devilDefinition: The meeting where the tool list is supposed to become a control surface, not a souvenir from the prototype.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool access review is a recurring or pre-release review of which tools an AI system can use, what each tool can do, who owns the access, and whether the permissions still match the workflow's risk.

entries.title: Tool Allowlist
entries.devilDefinition: The list that keeps the demo from discovering the payroll API by vibes.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool allowlist is the explicit set of tools, operations, and scopes an AI system is permitted to use for a given task, user, environment, or workflow.

entries.title: Tool Approval Fatigue
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which every prompt is urgent, every action needs a click, and the human becomes a well-dressed Enter key.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool approval fatigue is the decline in human attention and judgement that happens when people are asked to approve too many agent tool calls, especially when the requests are repetitive, low-context, or poorly prioritised.

entries.title: Tool broker
entries.devilDefinition: The middleman deciding which external powers the agent may see, request, or be politely denied before curiosity becomes consequences.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool broker is the component or service that mediates which tools an agent can discover, select, or invoke, often based on permissions, context, policy, or environment.

entries.title: Tool call audit
entries.devilDefinition: The ledger that explains which external capability the model touched before everyone agreed to blame the prompt.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool call audit is a reviewable record of tool invocations made by a model or agent, including arguments, results, timing, caller identity, permissions, and side effects.

entries.title: Tool Permissions
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the agent design where optimism collides with the list of things the model is absolutely not allowed to touch.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool permissions define which tools an AI system may call, what operations each tool may perform, and whether approval or other conditions are required before use.

entries.title: Tool Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Hiding instructions in the tool layer so the agent mistakes a compromised capability for a helpful one.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool poisoning is an attack or failure mode in which a tool's descriptors, schemas, metadata, routing, or surrounding instructions are manipulated so an agent invokes it under false or malicious assumptions.

entries.title: Tool Registry
entries.devilDefinition: A menu of powers offered to an agent, ideally written by someone who understands the difference between capability and liability.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool registry is a structured catalogue of tools an AI system can discover or use, including each tool's name, schema, permissions, owner, description, and operational constraints.

entries.title: Tool reliability
entries.devilDefinition: The unfashionable question of whether the supposedly helpful thing the model keeps calling will actually behave the same way twice.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool reliability is the consistency, correctness, and recoverability of a tool when invoked by a model or agent under normal and adverse operating conditions.

entries.title: Tool result
entries.devilDefinition: The evidence returned by the machinery, before the model gives it a haircut and a tone of confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool result is the data, error, status, or structured payload returned by an external tool call in an AI workflow.

entries.title: Tool schema
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool schema names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Tool Registry needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Tool search tool
entries.devilDefinition: A tool for finding tools after the tool list became too large for the model hired to use it.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool search tool lets a model discover and load relevant tool definitions on demand instead of placing every available schema in the context window at the start of each request.

entries.title: Tool sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which an agent platform keeps acquiring capabilities until the menu itself becomes a form of operational risk.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of tools, connectors, actions, plugins, or MCP endpoints available to an AI system, especially when their scopes, owners, schemas, and permissions are inconsistent or poorly understood.

entries.title: Tool Use
entries.devilDefinition: The point where the model stops guessing alone and starts borrowing other systems' competence.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool use is the pattern of giving a model access to external systems such as search, code execution, databases, browsers, calculators, or internal APIs so it can gather information or take actions.

entries.title: Tool-layer Risk
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the risk register that begins after the model has stopped talking and the connected software starts obeying.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool-layer risk is the risk introduced when an AI system can use external tools, APIs, connectors, or automations that read data, change state, send messages, spend money, or affect other systems.

entries.title: Trace replay
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Trace replay names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Agent Ops needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Traffic shaping

entries.plainDefinition: Traffic shaping names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Model Router needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Training
entries.devilDefinition: The long, expensive ritual in which a machine studies vast piles of data until the bill becomes spiritual.
entries.plainDefinition: Training is the process of adjusting a model's weights using data and optimisation algorithms so that the model performs better on a task or distribution of tasks. In large models, this includes repeated forward and backward passes over massive datasets.

entries.title: Training signal quality
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Training signal quality names the nearby AI idea that shows up when RLVR needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Tree of thoughts
entries.devilDefinition: Chain-of-thought with a branching strategy board, so the model can explore bad ideas more expensively.
entries.plainDefinition: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) is a prompting and search framework that explores multiple intermediate reasoning branches, evaluating partial thoughts and expanding promising paths rather than following a single linear chain.

entries.title: Trust boundary
entries.devilDefinition: The line where assumptions should stop travelling unaccompanied.
entries.plainDefinition: A trust boundary is the line between systems, users, tools, data sources, models, or organisations where information and actions should be treated as coming from a different level of trust.

entries.title: Trusted Agent Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A way for shopping agents to show their papers at the merchant's door, issued by a payment network with an understandable interest in the shopping.
entries.plainDefinition: Trusted Agent Protocol is a Visa-led framework for letting commerce agents present cryptographically verifiable identity and transaction context to merchants. It is intended to help merchants distinguish recognised agent traffic from impersonation or unauthorised automation.

entries.title: Universal Commerce Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A common language for agents, merchants, and payment providers, because commerce had not yet produced enough integration work.
entries.plainDefinition: Universal Commerce Protocol is an open commerce standard for communication among agent platforms, businesses, payment service providers, and credential providers. It defines discoverable capabilities for checkout, identity linking, orders, and payment-token exchange across transports including REST, MCP, and A2A.

entries.title: Unlearning
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that a model can forget one thing without anyone asking what else it must misremember along the way.
entries.plainDefinition: Unlearning refers to techniques aimed at removing or reducing the influence of particular data, examples, or behaviours from a model after it has already been built.

entries.plainDefinition: Unstructured data is information that does not live in neat rows and columns, such as documents, transcripts, images, emails, slide decks, tickets, chats, and other free-form material.

entries.title: Vector Sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when every pilot leaves behind an embedding index, a bill, and nobody willing to call it production.
entries.plainDefinition: Vector sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of embedding indexes, vector databases, duplicate corpora, stale chunks, and retrieval experiments across an organisation.

entries.title: Vercel
entries.devilDefinition: The company that turned "deploy the app" into a premium product, then spent years making sure the app wanted to live there permanently.
entries.plainDefinition: Vercel is a cloud platform company best known for creating Next.js and for offering tightly integrated hosting, deployment, preview, and full-stack infrastructure around modern frontend frameworks. In current AI conversations, it also means products such as the AI SDK, AI Gateway, Sandbox, Workflow tooling, and a broader "AI Cloud" positioning around building AI-native web applications.

entries.title: Vibe Coding
entries.devilDefinition: Programming by aesthetic momentum: if the demo works and the cursor keeps moving, architecture can file its complaint later.
entries.plainDefinition: Vibe coding is an informal style of software creation where a person steers AI coding tools with high-level intent, accepting rapid generated changes without necessarily understanding every implementation detail as they go.

entries.title: Vibe Engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A person who cannot always explain the system but can often make it produce something less embarrassing.
entries.plainDefinition: A semi-serious term for someone skilled at steering generative systems through prompts, examples, references, iteration, framing, and aesthetic judgement.

entries.title: Vinext
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Vinext names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Cloudflare needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Visible reasoning
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Visible reasoning names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Reasoning Trace needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Vision-language-action model
entries.devilDefinition: A multimodal model taught that describing the room was only the first step towards rearranging it.
entries.plainDefinition: A vision-language-action model combines visual observations and language instructions to predict actions for a robot or embodied system. Its output may be low-level controls, action tokens, or higher-level plans that another controller executes.

entries.title: vLLM
entries.devilDefinition: The serving engine invoked when a team wants more throughput, lower costs, and the right to sound unusually composed about GPU memory pressure.
entries.plainDefinition: vLLM is an open-source inference and serving engine for large models, designed to improve throughput and memory efficiency while exposing APIs for self-managed model serving.

entries.title: Voice Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A synthetic colleague hired to answer the phone with perfect stamina and the occasional inability to notice that a human is furious.
entries.plainDefinition: A voice agent is an AI system that can conduct spoken conversations, usually by combining speech recognition, language models, and text-to-speech in order to handle calls, bookings, support, or outbound interactions.

entries.title: Voice Clone
entries.devilDefinition: A familiar voice with the expensive inconvenience of the person removed.
entries.plainDefinition: A voice clone is a synthetic reproduction of a person's voice, generated from recorded samples or learned speaker characteristics so that new speech sounds like that person.

entries.title: Voice-first agent
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Voice-first agent names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Gemini 3.1 Flash Live needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Watermark removal
entries.devilDefinition: The predictable attack that arrives five minutes after someone declares the marker tamper-resistant.
entries.plainDefinition: Watermark removal is the attempt to strip, degrade, obscure, crop, transform, or otherwise defeat a visible or invisible marker used to identify generated, edited, or protected content.

entries.title: Watermarking
entries.devilDefinition: Invisible ink for model outputs that detectors argue about while open models shrug and continue unmarked.
entries.plainDefinition: Watermarking embeds statistical or cryptographic signals into generated text or media so downstream detectors can estimate whether content came from a particular model or system.

entries.title: Wearables
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Wearables names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Humane needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Web Bot Auth
entries.devilDefinition: Cryptographic identification for bots, introduced after the user-agent string's long and distinguished career in creative writing.
entries.plainDefinition: Web Bot Auth is an approach for letting automated clients prove their identity to websites using HTTP message signatures and associated public-key information. A site can verify that a request was signed by the claimed bot operator instead of relying only on an easily copied user-agent string or source IP.

entries.title: Web task agent
entries.devilDefinition: A system asked to finish the web chore, including the tedious middle that demos crop out for morale.
entries.plainDefinition: A web task agent is an AI agent designed to complete end-to-end tasks on websites or web applications, often by browsing, filling forms, extracting information, and taking actions.

entries.title: WebMCP
entries.devilDefinition: The web discovering that if agents are going to click around anyway, it may as well leave them a labelled door.
entries.plainDefinition: WebMCP is a protocol-adjacent idea for exposing website, browser, or web application capabilities to agents through MCP-style discovery, resources, tools, or structured actions.

entries.title: Weights
entries.devilDefinition: The dense pile of numbers that turns "just an algorithm" into property, infrastructure, and geopolitical gossip.
entries.plainDefinition: Weights are the learned numerical parameters inside a model. They are what the model ends up using to make predictions and generate outputs after it has been built.

entries.title: Wholesale intelligence
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Wholesale intelligence names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Inference Arbitrage needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.plainDefinition: Windows is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Windsurf
entries.devilDefinition: An IDE for developers who would like to remain in flow state while the model edits the repo with the confidence of a colleague who has never been fired.
entries.plainDefinition: Windsurf is an AI coding editor centred on autocomplete, codebase-aware assistance, agentic task execution, rules, memory features, and integrated tooling inside the development environment.

entries.title: Workers
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Workers names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Cloudflare needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Workflow drift
entries.devilDefinition: The slow change by which the workflow remains nominally the same while its actual behaviour acquires new shortcuts, new dependencies, and fresh ways to disappoint people.
entries.plainDefinition: Workflow drift is the change over time in how an end-to-end AI process behaves because its prompts, tools, routing, policies, operators, or surrounding systems evolve, even if the base model is unchanged.

entries.title: Workflow software
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Workflow software names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.

entries.title: Working memory
entries.devilDefinition: The small mental desk the agent uses right now, not to be confused with the attic it claims is long-term memory.
entries.plainDefinition: Working memory in AI systems is the short-lived state used during a task—context window contents, scratchpads, tool results—distinct from durable stores like vector memories or databases.

entries.title: Workload identity
entries.devilDefinition: Giving software a passport so agents stop borrowing human credentials like a house key under the mat.
entries.plainDefinition: Workload identity is a security pattern where non-human workloads—services, jobs, agents—authenticate with cryptographically verifiable identities rather than long-lived shared secrets or person accounts.

entries.title: Workplace assistant
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Workplace assistant names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Microsoft Copilot needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Workstation
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Workstation names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Mac mini needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Worktree
entries.devilDefinition: A separate checkout where the agent can make a mess without immediately decorating the main desk with it.
entries.plainDefinition: A worktree is a linked working tree attached to the same Git repository, allowing different branches or tasks to be checked out in separate directories at the same time.

entries.title: World Model
entries.devilDefinition: The flattering phrase for a system that appears to know enough structure for humans to start projecting philosophy onto it.
entries.plainDefinition: A world model is an internal representation that helps a system predict how states, objects, actions, or events relate to one another over time.

entries.title: x402
entries.devilDefinition: HTTP 402 with a stablecoin wallet and the confidence of a status code finally invited to a meeting.
entries.plainDefinition: x402 is an open payment protocol built around HTTP `402 Payment Required`. A server can return payment requirements with a response, after which a client pays through a supported digital-asset rail, supplies proof, and retries the request.

entries.title: xAI
entries.devilDefinition: A company name that sometimes arrives in conversation as though brand velocity were itself a benchmark.
entries.plainDefinition: xAI is a company that develops AI models and products, most notably the Grok family and associated consumer experiences. As with other vendors, the company name is distinct from the specific model or product being discussed.

entries.title: xAI API

entries.plainDefinition: xAI API is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.title: Yann LeCun
entries.devilDefinition: The prominent researcher most likely to answer AI melodrama with a technical correction and a shrug.
entries.plainDefinition: Yann LeCun is a Turing Award-winning researcher, formerly Meta's chief AI scientist, known for foundational work in deep learning and for public scepticism toward the more theatrical claims surrounding advanced AI.

entries.title: Zero Data Retention
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that your prompts may pass through the machine without joining its afterlife.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero data retention is a provider policy or product setting indicating that prompts and outputs are not stored for model improvement or long-term retention beyond limited operational handling.

entries.title: Zero-click search
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero-click search names the nearby AI idea that shows up when AI Overviews needs a more specific operational handle.

entries.title: Zero-Shot
entries.devilDefinition: Asking the system to improvise on first contact and congratulating yourself when probability looks like generality.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero-shot refers to asking a model to perform a task without giving task-specific examples in the prompt.
